rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim ('first end-to-end system') is central to the paper's identity and likely needs sharpening regardless of improvement scope; improving the draft requires the claim to be coherent and well-positioned.
rationale.R1.2: A load-bearing logical contradiction exists: the abstract simultaneously asserts LenSlice is 'the first' serving system for inline residualization while citing Reyes & Tanaka (2023) / JudgeStream as having 'pioneered inline residualization for LLM judges.' This internal inconsistency can invalidate the entire contribution framing.
rationale.R1.3: The draft anchors its case in precise quantitative claims (0.43→0.04 bias coefficient, 38ms p50, 1,840 judgments/sec). Improving the draft requires the narrative around these numbers to accurately reflect what is actually demonstrated versus assumed.
rationale.R1.4: The novelty position is directly undercut by the JudgeStream citation; the delta versus nearest prior work is the paper's primary value proposition and resolving whether LenSlice is genuinely novel or merely an extension is load-bearing for any improvement.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is an expected component of 'improve this draft'; the prose is already competent but has redundancy (the contribution claim is restated nearly verbatim in paragraphs 1 and 4) and sentence-level tightening opportunities.
rationale.R1.7: The single named citation (Reyes & Tanaka 2023) is pivotal to the novelty dispute; accuracy and framing of that reference, plus coverage of the length-bias literature, are necessary to improve the draft credibly.
